questions.stem: The nurse is caring for a patient 4 days after bowel surgery. The patient has not passed flatus, bowel sounds are hypoactive in all quadrants, and the abdomen is mildly distended. Vital signs are stable. The nurse CORRECTLY identifies this presentation as:

options.text: Paralytic ileus — expected post-operative finding
Small bowel obstruction requiring immediate NG tube decompression
Peritonitis requiring emergent surgical consultation
Large bowel obstruction requiring CT scan and surgical consult

questions.rationale: Paralytic ileus (adynamic ileus) is an expected complication in the first 3–5 days after abdominal or bowel surgery, caused by handling of the bowel and anesthetic effects. It presents with absent or hypoactive bowel sounds, lack of flatus, and mild distension without severe pain, fever, or hemodynamic instability. Mechanical obstruction (B, D) presents with high-pitched borborygmi, crampy pain, and vomiting — not simply hypoactive sounds. Peritonitis (C) would have fever, rigid abdomen, and rebound tenderness.

distractorRationales.b: Small bowel obstruction requiring immediate NG tube decompression misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced pain points to Paralytic ileus — expected post-operative finding, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Peritonitis requiring emergent surgical consultation misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced pain points to Paralytic ileus — expected post-operative finding, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Large bowel obstruction requiring CT scan and surgical consult misses the clinical pattern: Medical Surgical Advanced pain points to Paralytic ileus — expected post-operative finding, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Ileus vs obstruction: ileus = absent/hypoactive sounds, mild distension, expected post-op; obstruction = high-pitched sounds, crampy pain, vomiting.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A client with depression says: 'I don't see the point in trying anymore. Nothing I do matters.' Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.'
'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.'
'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.'
'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.'

questions.rationale: Option B is an open-ended exploration that invites the client to elaborate, demonstrating active listening without judgment. It also screens for suicidal ideation — 'nothing I do matters' and 'no point in trying' may signal passive suicidal thinking. Option A uses false reassurance ('so much to live for') and assumes understanding without exploring further. Option C minimizes the client's experience ('everyone feels that way'). Option D is non-therapeutic confrontation that dismisses the client's feelings.

distractorRationales.a: 'I understand. Sometimes things feel hopeless, but you have so much to live for.' misses the clinical pattern: Therapeutic Communication safety points to 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.', so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: 'Everyone feels that way sometimes. It will get better soon.' misses the clinical pattern: Therapeutic Communication safety points to 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.', so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: 'You shouldn't think like that. Let's focus on the positive things in your life.' misses the clinical pattern: Therapeutic Communication safety points to 'Tell me more about what you mean when you say nothing matters.', so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication: use open-ended statements to explore meaning, especially when statements may signal suicidal ideation.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with pneumonia who is receiving 4L/min via nasal cannula. The SpO2 is 91%. The nurse repositions the patient and administers the scheduled nebulizer treatment. Which finding would indicate the interventions were effective?

options.text: Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%
The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television
Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer
The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min

questions.rationale: Effective respiratory interventions produce measurable objective improvements in gas exchange: SpO2 rising to ≥ 94-95% and respiratory rate normalizing toward < 20/min indicate improved ventilation and oxygenation. Decreased anxiety may reflect improved oxygenation but is subjective and non-specific. Increased breath sounds after a nebulizer can indicate mobilization of secretions (which is initially normal), not necessarily a therapeutic response. Unchanged SpO2 and RR indicate no meaningful improvement from the interventions.

distractorRationales.b: The patient reports less anxiety and requests to watch television misses the clinical pattern: Respiratory / Oxygenation ventilation points to Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Adventitious breath sounds increase bilaterally after the nebulizer misses the clinical pattern: Respiratory / Oxygenation ventilation points to Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: The patient's skin color remains unchanged and RR remains at 24/min misses the clinical pattern: Respiratory / Oxygenation ventilation points to Respiratory rate decreases from 24 to 18 breaths/min and SpO2 increases to 95%, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.
visualRationale.conclusion: Evaluate respiratory interventions with objective data: SpO2 trend, RR normalization, and work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring). Subjective comfort is supportive but secondary.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80 misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing tachycardia points to HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48 misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing tachycardia points to HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72 misses the clinical pattern: Pediatric Nursing tachycardia points to HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care aspiration points to Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.b: Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care aspiration points to Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk misses the clinical pattern: Perioperative Care aspiration points to Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Insulin Safety hypoglycemia points to Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Insulin Safety hypoglycemia points to Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh misses the clinical pattern: Pharmacology / Insulin Safety hypoglycemia points to Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10 misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due misses the clinical pattern: Safety And Infection Control / Prioritization infection points to A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.

questions.stem: A patient with severe preeclampsia at 34 weeks develops an eclamptic seizure. She is on magnesium sulfate. Which intervention takes priority during the active seizure?

options.text: Administer IV diazepam 5 mg to stop the seizure
Insert a padded tongue blade to protect the airway
Position on her left side, protect from injury, administer oxygen, and note seizure duration
Give a magnesium sulfate bolus of 4 g IV immediately

questions.rationale: During an eclamptic seizure: protect the patient from injury, position on left lateral decubitus (reduces aortocaval compression, improves uteroplacental blood flow), apply oxygen via face mask, and time the seizure. IV diazepam (A) is not first-line; magnesium is the drug of choice for eclampsia treatment and prevention of recurrent seizures. A padded tongue blade (B) should never be inserted during an active seizure — high risk of injury to teeth and nurse. A magnesium bolus (D) may be given post-seizure if patient is not already at therapeutic levels, but protecting the patient during the active seizure comes first.

distractorRationales.a: Administer IV diazepam 5 mg to stop the seizure misses the clinical pattern: Severe Preeclampsia oxygen points to Position on her left side, protect from injury, administer oxygen, and note seizure duration, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.b: Insert a padded tongue blade to protect the airway misses the clinical pattern: Severe Preeclampsia oxygen points to Position on her left side, protect from injury, administer oxygen, and note seizure duration, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Give a magnesium sulfate bolus of 4 g IV immediately misses the clinical pattern: Severe Preeclampsia oxygen points to Position on her left side, protect from injury, administer oxygen, and note seizure duration, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Eclamptic seizure priority: protect from injury, left lateral position, O2, time seizure — never insert anything into the mouth during a seizure.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40 misses the clinical pattern: Community Health cardiac points to Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture misses the clinical pattern: Community Health cardiac points to Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps misses the clinical pattern: Community Health cardiac points to Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse has four patients to assess. Which patient should the nurse see FIRST, applying Maslow's hierarchy of needs?

options.text: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure
A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%
A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home
A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10

questions.rationale: Maslow's hierarchy places physiological needs at the foundation. Oxygenation is the highest physiological priority. A patient with RR 8 and SpO2 88% is in respiratory distress — an immediate life-threatening concern that takes absolute priority. Anxiety (A) is a safety/psychological need. Discharge teaching (C) is a self-actualization/cognitive need. Pain rated 4/10 (D) is physiological but not immediately life-threatening compared to respiratory failure.

distractorRationales.a: A patient who is anxious about an upcoming procedure misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals oxygenation points to A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: A patient who needs discharge teaching before going home misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals oxygenation points to A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: A patient asking for pain medication rated 4/10 misses the clinical pattern: Critical Care Fundamentals oxygenation points to A patient with a respiratory rate of 8 breaths/min and SpO2 of 88%, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Maslow hierarchy on NCLEX: airway/breathing/circulation (physiological) always come before safety, love/belonging, esteem, and self-actualization.
